summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PARENTING – Ex Tempore Reasons – Recovery order application – Where there are final orders in place for the children to live with the mother and spend time with the father – Where the father has failed to facilitate the return of the children to the mother and comply with previous orders – Where the Court previously refused the father's application to reopen the parenting proceedings – Where the children are at risk as a consequence of the actions by the father to undermine the parenting arrangements – Recovery order made to lie on the file – Injunction made restraining the father from communicating with the children for a period of three months.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

KARI J:

INTRODUCTION

These proceedings come before the Court today in relation to the parenting arrangements for the parties' two children. Those children are X, born in 2012, and Y, born in 2015. The parenting application before the Court today was made by the mother on 25 March 2026. By that Initiating Application, the mother asks the Court to make mirror orders, both by way of interlocutory orders and final orders.
Of significance, the principal focus of the application that has been filed by the mother, is that she asks the Court to make a recovery order which has the effect of providing for the Adelaide Federal Police (“AFP”) to remove the children from wherever they are presently located and to recover and return them to the mother. That application is opposed by the father, and he filed a Response to an Initiating Application to that effect on 31 March 2026, being earlier today. Those documents were filed in accordance with orders of this Court made on 27 March 2026, in circumstances where the mother's Initiating Application was referred to my chambers and a determination was made that the mother's application, as requested by her, would be heard on an urgent basis.
In addition, the Court has the assistance of an Independent Children's Lawyer (“ICL”) today. It might seem surprising that an Independent Children's Lawyer is present, given the Initiating Application was only filed on 25 March 2026. However, that an Independent Children's Lawyer is present, is as a consequence of the very long history of these proceedings, but ostensibly as a result of orders made by this Court on 5 March 2026, which provided for the Independent Children's Lawyer to remain appointed for the children in these proceedings as and from the expiration of 28 days from the orders of 5 March 2026 and/or subject to any appeal in relation to those orders.
As is obvious, the 28 day period has not yet expired and the Court notes that there has been no appeal filed in relation to the orders of 5 March 2026. I record that these reasons are being delivered at 7.10 pm in circumstances where this Court has sat out of hours to hear and determine the applications presently before the Court, such was the urgency determined appropriate.
As permitted by s 69ZL of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”), I intend to give short form reasons in relation to the parenting orders that I propose to make today.

BACKGROUND

These proceedings have, as I indicated earlier, a very long history before the Court. I do not propose, for the purposes of these reasons, to traverse that history in any detail whatsoever. The history is traversed more thoroughly in the reasons published by the Court on 5 March 2026 (Tyler & Cadmon (No 3) [2026] FedCFamC1F 103), together with the reasons published by Jarrett J on 7 August 2025 (Tyler & Cadmon [2025] FedCFamC1F 389).
All that is sufficient for the Court to presently record is that, following the conclusion of a trial heard by Jarrett J, his Honour published final orders on 7 August 2025. Amongst other things, those orders provided for the children to transition from the primary care of the father into the primary care of the mother. For reasons which I will not go into for present purposes, those orders were not carried into effect.
Ultimately, further proceedings were commenced by the father, in which he asked the Court to revisit the parenting arrangements for the children. The mother opposed the father doing so, and those issues were determined by me in the orders that were ultimately made by the Court on 5 March 2026. The orders made that day dismissed the parenting applications that had been made by the father and were then before the Court and concluded those proceedings.
Of significance for present purposes today, the Court made a declaratory order on 5 March 2026 that the final parenting orders made on 7 August 2025 in relation to both children remained in full force and effect (Order 2).
Importantly, injunctions were also made on 5 March 2026 (Order 6), restraining the father from the following:
(a) removing the children from the mother's care whatsoever and/or from court childcare this day and/or permitting any other person to do so;

(b) attending at the children's school and/or permitting any other person to do so; and

(c) communicating with the children in any way, either directly or indirectly, or permitting any other person to do so, save as provided by Order 10 of the orders made 7 August 2025.

I pause at this juncture to record the terms of Order 10 made on 7 August 2025.
That order provided that the mother, facilitate Skype calls between the children and the father, as follows:
(a) each Sunday between 8.30 pm and 9.30 pm Adelaide time (ACST);

(b) each alternate Wednesday between 6.30 am and 7.00 am (ACST); and

(c) at such other times as requested by the children.

Those orders are significant for a number of reasons, but principally because, during the course of the hearing today, a number of oral applications have been made. The mother, today, asks the Court to make an order suspending Order 10 made on 7 August 2025 for a period of three months. In addition, an application has been made by the Independent Children's Lawyer for certain mandatory injunctions to be made directed to the father. Those injunctions would provide for:
(a) The father to be restrained from facilitating paying for the children to have any mobile phone number, the father having provided two separate mobile phone numbers for the children, and/or any other mobile phone accounts;

(b) Secondly, that the father block the children's mobile phone numbers from his device;

(c) Thirdly, that the father provide the mother with passwords for the children's phones and iPads and associated devices; and

(d) Fourthly, that the father disable any applications installed on his devices that indicate where the children might be at any given time.

In relation to the additional orders that have been promoted during today's hearing, the father opposes any of those orders being made. Suffice to say, it is the father's position that no orders should be made to facilitate or ensure that the orders of 7 August 2025 will remain in full force and effect.
While not stated in express terms, it is the father's position that the current arrangements which now see the children not living with the mother despite the orders of 7 August 2025 providing for that to occur, should remain and there should be an ongoing cloud hanging over the children in terms of their parenting arrangements.
I say that because, as part of the orders made by the Court on 5 March 2026, a harmful proceedings order was made, directed to the father, pursuant to s 102QAC of the Act (Order 7). As a consequence of that order, the father is prohibited, without leave of the Court, from instituting further proceedings. At this juncture, the Court, nor the mother, nor the Independent Children's Lawyer, appear aware as to whether any further proceedings have been attempted to be instituted by the father.
Understanding that very brief summary of the more recent history of these proceedings, it is important to understand, and the Court has had regard to the totality of the reasons delivered by Jarrett J on 7 August 2025, together with the reasons published by me on 5 March 2026.

DOCUMENTS BEFORE THE COURT

I also record, for present purposes, that regard has been had, during the course of today's hearing for the purposes of the present applications, to a number of documents. Those documents on behalf of the mother include:
(a) the Initiating Application filed 25 March 2026;

(b) the Notice of Child Abuse, Family Violence or Risk filed 25 March 2026;

(c) the mother’s affidavit filed 25 March 2026;

(d) the Genuine Steps Certificate filed 25 March 2026; and

(e) the mother’s affidavit regarding the non-filing of family dispute resolution certificate filed 25 March 2026.

In addition, the Court has had regard to the tranche of documents filed by the father in support of the orders that he promotes today. Those documents are:
(a) the Response to Initiating Application filed 31 March 2026,

(b) the Genuine Steps Certificate filed 31 March 2026;

(c) the Notice of Child Abuse, Family Violence or Risk filed 31 March 2026; and

(d) the father’s affidavit filed 31 March 2026.

In addition, the Court has had regard to a number of additional documents.
Those documents include Exhibit RO-M1, which is an SA Ambulance Service Clinical Notes Supplement dated March 2026, that exhibit being tendered during the course of today's hearing and received without objection by either the Independent Children's Lawyer or the father.
The Court has additionally had regard to responses received by the Court, as a result of orders made in the proceedings pursuant to s 67ZBD of the Act, directed to both the Department of Child Protection and South Australian Police. The Department of Child Protection document is that dated March 2026, and the SAPOL document is dated March 2026.
Both of those documents, in response to the s 67ZBD orders made by the Court, were released to the parties and the Independent Children's Lawyer, pursuant to orders made by the Court earlier today. At the commencement of today's hearing, the parties and the Independent Children's Lawyer acknowledged that they had received those documents and had the opportunity to consider the same.
Should there be any doubt, the Court has had regard to all of the material filed and relied upon by each of the parties for the purposes of today's hearing, together with the submissions that have been made during the course of the hearing today. It should not be taken by the parties that my failure to refer specifically to any of that material means that I have not considered that material. As I make clear, I am giving short reasons so that I am able to deal with the matter today.

BEST INTERESTS CONSIDERATIONS

The Court is mindful that when making any parenting orders, orders ought be made that have the children's best interest as the paramount concern. In these proceedings, the Court has already considered these children's best interests.
They were considered when his Honour Jarrett J delivered reasons and made orders on 7 August 2025, and they were considered afresh on 5 March 2026, when orders and reasons were published by me. The cumulative effect of the orders made on 7 August 2025 and 5 March 2026 is that the orders made on 7 August 2025 are those which the Court unequivocally considered to be in the children's best interests. Indeed, only a few short weeks ago, the Court refused the father's application to reopen the parenting proceedings.
Of some significance is that the Court has already made findings as to the risk that these children have suffered and are at risk of continuing to suffer, as a consequence of the actions, for present purposes of significance, by the father.
It is of some significant concern to the Court that, despite the orders made on 5 March 2026, it would appear, and the Court apprehends, that the father has taken very significant steps to undermine the orders of the Court and undermine the parenting arrangements that this Court implemented in the children's best interests.
The steps that appear to have been taken since the orders of 5 March 2026, which will be the subject of determination on a later date in circumstances where the mother has foreshadowed the filing of a Contravention Application, include, but are not limited to, the father failing to comply with the injunctions set out in Order 6 made on 5 March 2026, as earlier identified. They also include the father's failure to comply with Order 10 made on 7 August 2025, limiting the periods of time that the father is permitted to have communication with the children.
Significantly, from the mother's perspective, she asserts, and the Court apprehends, that the father has, indeed, significantly undermined the children's stability in the mother's care. The reasons for the Court apprehending that that may well be the case arises as a consequence of the material the father has filed and the admissions that he has made, both in his filed material and during today's hearing. Those admissions include that he has continued communicating with the children following the orders of 5 March 2026, be that by way of text message or otherwise.
The admissions also include that the father has facilitated the children coming into his care, in the case of the child, Y, on 23 March 2026, and in relation to the child, X, while she is not presently in the father's care, she is not in the care of the mother as the final orders contemplated, and that she has since 17 March 2026 been in the care of the paternal grandmother.
The Court is also concerned that it appears that the father has, at all times, understood the whereabouts of these children, both as a consequence of an AirTag in at least one of the children's bags taken to the mother's home, but also as a consequence of applications installed on the children's devices.
Whilst the father tells the Court that he has the children's best interests at heart, the father, on any view at this juncture from the Court's perspective, has acted in ways, and I find that he has acted in ways, to undermine the parenting orders made on 7 August 2025. Whilst the father says that his actions are in response to the children and with the children's best interests at heart, the Court apprehends that the father's conduct since the making, certainly of the orders of 5 March 2026, has only added, as he acknowledged during the course of the hearing today, to heighten the children's distress.
Those actions have included requesting or facilitating welfare checks upon the children at the mother's home following the children coming into her care on 5 March 2026, concerningly within at least two hours of the children returning home with the mother.
I have already commented as to the Court's concerns as to the role of the father's communication with the children in relation to heightening their distress. It strikes me that where the Court has already made findings as to what arrangements are in these children's best interests, the options available to the Court, in the absence of fresh parenting proceedings being commenced, is that orders ought be made to facilitate the final orders made on 7 August 2025, when the Court heard and determined those arrangements considered to be in the best interests of the children.
For all of those reasons, it is appropriate and in the children's best interests that a recovery order be made today. Orders will be made that provide for the recovery order to lie on the file because the Court apprehends, as his Jarrett J commented at [147] of the reasons he published on 7 August 2025 and as recent events appear to bear out:
The applicant [father] will not abide by the terms of any orders of the court. He has a consistent track record of non-compliance.
The Court apprehends that this track-record of non-compliance is ongoing and that the fears held by Jarrett J continue to be held by the Court.
During the course of today's hearing, the Independent Children's Lawyer made submissions of very real concern in relation to the impact of the father's behaviour upon the children. It is my observation, as a result of the material filed by the father in relation to today's application, but also having heard his submissions today, that he appears to continue to have no insight as to the impact of his behaviour upon his children and/or, if he does have some insight, it is his determined and considered view that he will proceed, regardless of any harmful impact of his behaviour upon the children. That is to say that he promotes his wishes above the needs of the children and the orders of this Court.
For all of those reasons, as I say, a recovery order will be made and that order will lie on the file so that the mother is able to have the children recovered to her care without the need for further orders of the Court.
In addition, the Court considers that as a result of the admissions made by the father both in his material and during submissions today as to his communication with the children following the making of the orders of 5 March 2026, that it would be in the children's best interest to promote their stability and to give them a chance to settle in the care of the mother without further emotional, psychological or other harm being effected upon them, that the additional orders that had been promoted today also be made.
Those orders will include an order suspending Order 10 made on 7 August 2025 for a period of three months. They will also include the mandatory injunctions that have been promoted jointly by the Independent Children's Lawyer and the mother today.
In addition, the Independent Children's Lawyer has promoted an extension of the appointment of the Independent Children's Lawyer for a period of approximately six months. That is an order that I am also persuaded to make.
I am persuaded to make those orders, given the history of the litigation that has prevailed since his Honour made final orders on 7 August 2025 and in circumstances where the Court apprehends, sadly, that the father is unlikely to comply with orders of this Court on an ongoing basis. While the Court remains ever hopeful that the father, at some point, will accept the authority of the orders made by this Court, at this juncture, there is nothing that has occurred in the history of the litigation to give the Court comfort that he shall do so. Accordingly, it is appropriate to continue the appointment of the Independent Children's Lawyer.
NOTE:

These reasons were delivered ex tempore and have been settled from the transcript. Grammatical errors have been corrected, citations have been added and amendments have been made to make the orally delivered reasons clearer and easier to read.
